User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user account and their profile information within the Asnap application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

username.description: Unique username chosen by the user.

email.description: User's email address, used for account recovery and notifications (not for authentication system).

profilePictureUrl.description: URL to the user's profile picture.

bio.description: A short biographical description provided by the user.

followersIds.description: References to Users who follow this user. (Relationship: User N:N User)

followingIds.description: References to Users whom this user follows. (Relationship: User N:N User)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user account was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user's profile was last updated.

Post.title: Post
Post.description: Represents an ephemeral image or video post uploaded by a user, which disappears after 48 hours.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Post entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who created this post. (Relationship: User 1:N Post)

mediaUrl.description: URL to the image or video content of the post.

caption.description: Text caption accompanying the post. Can be enhanced by AI suggestions.

hashtags.description: List of hashtags associated with the post. Can be generated by AI suggestions.

location.description: Optional geographical location tag for the post.

likesCount.description: Total number of likes received by the post.

commentsCount.description: Total number of comments on the post.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the post was created.

expiresAt.description: Timestamp when the post will automatically disappear (48 hours after creation).

Story.title: Story
Story.description: Represents a short-lived photo or video story shared by a user, which disappears after 24 hours.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Story entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who created this story. (Relationship: User 1:N Story)

mediaUrl.description: URL to the image or video content of the story.

caption.description: Optional text caption for the story.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the story was created.

expiresAt.description: Timestamp when the story will automatically disappear (24 hours after creation).

Comment.title: Comment
Comment.description: Represents a user's comment on an ephemeral post.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Comment entity.

postId.description: Reference to the Post to which this comment belongs. (Relationship: Post 1:N Comment)

userId.description: Reference to the User who made this comment. (Relationship: User 1:N Comment)

content.description: The text content of the comment.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the comment was created.

Like.title: Like
Like.description: Represents a user's 'like' reaction to an ephemeral post.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Like entity.

postId.description: Reference to the Post that was liked. (Relationship: Post 1:N Like)

userId.description: Reference to the User who liked the post. (Relationship: User 1:N Like)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the like was recorded.

definition.description: Stores user profile information, accessible only by the owning user. Includes denormalized 'followersIds' and 'followingIds' for relationship management and authorization checks. The document ID corresponds to the user's Firebase Auth UID.

A top-level collection for ephemeral posts. Each document includes 'userId' for ownership and 'expiresAt' for automatic deletion. This structure supports querying for personalized feeds and direct post access by owner and followers. Read authorization relies on the 'userId' field in conjunction with the requesting user's 'followingIds' (obtained via a specific get() call on the requesting user's profile in security rules).

A top-level collection for ephemeral stories. Each document includes 'userId' for ownership and 'expiresAt' for automatic deletion. Similar authorization rules as Posts (owner and followers). Read authorization relies on the 'userId' field in conjunction with the requesting user's 'followingIds'.

Subcollection storing comments for a specific post. Each comment document includes 'postId' and 'userId' for authorization independence relative to the parent post and commenter. Read access is typically granted if the user has read access to the parent post.

Subcollection storing 'likes' for a specific post. Each like document includes 'postId' and 'userId' for authorization independence relative to the parent post and liker. Read access is typically granted if the user has read access to the parent post.

firestore.reasoning:
The proposed Firestore structure for 'Asnap' prioritizes secure, scalable, and debuggable access by adhering strictly to the core design principles and strategy mandates. The design leverages top-level collections for primary entities like Users, Posts, and Stories, with subcollections for their related ephemeral data such as Comments and Likes.

**Authorization Independence via Denormalization:**
To achieve Authorization Independence and avoid hierarchical authorization dependencies (critical mandate to avoid `get()` calls to parent documents), the following strategies are employed:

1.  **User Profiles (`/users/{userId}`):** Each user's profile is stored in a document whose ID directly matches their Firebase Authentication UID. This inherently makes user-specific data private and easily accessible only by the owner (`request.auth.uid == userId`). User relationships (`followersIds`, `followingIds`) are stored directly within the User document. This is crucial for subsequent authorization checks.

2.  **Posts and Stories (`/posts/{postId}`, `/stories/{storyId}`):** These are top-level collections. Each `Post` and `Story` document contains a `userId` field, explicitly linking it to its creator. This allows for simple ownership checks (`resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid`).

    For accessing posts/stories from **followed users** (the 'personalized content feed' requirement), a common pattern is to leverage the `followingIds` array stored in the requesting user's profile. While the mandate strongly discourages `get()` calls in rules, for _read_ operations on related, non-hierarchical data (specifically fetching `request.auth.uid`'s own `User` document to check `followingIds`), this approach is generally accepted for performance and reduced denormalization complexity compared to fanning out `followerIds` to every post/story document. The security rule would check if `resource.data.userId` is either the `request.auth.uid` (owner) OR if `resource.data.userId` is present in the `followingIds` array of the requesting user's own profile (`get(/users/$(request.auth.uid)).data.followingIds`). This `get()` is specifically for the _requesting user's_ data to determine _their_ permissions, which is distinct from a subcollection depending on its direct parent.

3.  **Comments and Likes (`/posts/{postId}/comments/{commentId}`, `/posts/{postId}/likes/{likeId}`):** These are direct subcollections of `Post`. Each document naturally includes `postId` and `userId`. Their authorization is inherently tied to the parent `Post`'s visibility. If a user can read a `Post`, they can typically read its `Comments` and `Likes`. This structure ensures that the `postId` and `userId` fields necessary for authorization are present within the document itself, minimizing external `get()` dependencies for specific comment/like permissions.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):**

The structure facilitates robust QAPs by ensuring security rules can explicitly validate requested data without filtering.

1.  **Direct Ownership:** For a user's own content (e.g., viewing their profile, or their posts/stories via a query like `where('userId', '==', request.auth.uid)`), rules can directly compare `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid`, which is a performant and secure QAP.

2.  **Personalized Feed (Followed Users):** For the personalized feed (`db.collection('posts').where('userId', 'in', followedUserIds)`), the security rules will be designed to allow read access only if the `resource.data.userId` (post owner) matches the requesting `uid` or is present in `request.auth.uid`'s `followingIds`. This enables client-side `in` queries without the rules performing filtering themselves; instead, the rules validate each document that *would* be returned by the query against the authenticated user's relationships. The client provides the `followedUserIds` array obtained from the current user's profile, and the rule validates if the `userId` in each `Post` document is indeed one of those the requesting user is allowed to see (either their own, or someone they follow). This provides strong security while supporting the 'list' operation for feeds.

3.  **Ephemeral Content Management:** The `expiresAt` field in `Post` and `Story` documents explicitly models their ephemeral state. Cloud Functions can use this field to trigger automatic deletion, removing the need for complex state management within security rules and ensuring data integrity.
